questions.correctText: V_0 (1 minus e to negative t over RC)
questions.explanation: Charging capacitor voltage is V(t) = V_0*(1 - exp(-t/RC)), starting at 0 and approaching V_0 (the source voltage) exponentially. AP Physics C E&M derives this from the ODE R*(dQ/dt) + Q/C = EMF, with initial Q(0) = 0. Solution: Q(t) = C*EMF*(1 - exp(-t/RC)), and V_cap(t) = Q/C = EMF*(1 - exp(-t/RC)). At t = RC: V = EMF*(1 - 1/e) = 0.632*EMF. The 2018 FRQ tested this with numerical values. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct ODE solution.

choices: V_0 (1 minus e to negative t over RC)
V_0 e to negative t over RC
questions.correctText: V_0 e to negative t over RC
questions.explanation: Discharging capacitor voltage is V(t) = V_0*exp(-t/RC), starting at V_0 and decaying to zero exponentially. AP Physics C E&M derives this from the ODE R*(dQ/dt) + Q/C = 0 (no source), with initial Q(0) = Q_0. Solution: Q(t) = Q_0*exp(-t/RC), V(t) = Q/C = V_0*exp(-t/RC). The 2017 FRQ on a flash-photo capacitor (V_0 = 200 V) discharging into a flash bulb tested this. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards exponential-decay analysis; Practice 3 (graphical analysis) rewards reading tau from a V-t plot.

questions.prompt: Force on moving charge in B.
questions.explanation: The Lorentz force F = q*v x B is the magnetic force on a charge q moving with velocity v through a magnetic field B. Named for Hendrik Lorentz (1853-1928). AP Physics C E&M uses this for charged-particle motion in magnetic fields: cyclotrons, mass spectrometers, velocity selectors, plasma confinement. The 2018 FRQ #2 tested F = qv x B on a proton in a uniform B. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct cross-product computation; Practice 1 (representations) demands consistent right-hand-rule conventions.

Lorentz force formula is F = q*v x B (vector form), with magnitude F = |q|*v*B*sin(theta) where theta is the angle between v and B. AP Physics C E&M uses both forms: vector for direction (right-hand rule), magnitude for numerical computation. Maximum force at theta = 90 degrees; zero at theta = 0 or 180 (parallel motion). The 2019 FRQ tested cyclotron motion: a particle entering B perpendicular feels maximum force and moves in a circle. Science Practice 1 (representations) demands the right-hand rule applied correctly.

The SI unit of magnetic field is the tesla (T), equal to 1 N/(A*m) or 1 kg/(A*s^2). Named for Nikola Tesla (1856-1943). AP Physics C E&M uses tesla and the smaller gauss (1 T = 10^4 gauss). Earth's magnetic field is about 5 x 10^-5 T (0.5 gauss); a refrigerator magnet ~0.01 T; an MRI scanner 1-3 T; lab supermagnets 10-20 T; neutron-star magnetar fields 10^11 T. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) demands correct dimensional handling; Practice 4 (data analysis) rewards reading field magnitudes in real-world contexts.

Force on a current-carrying wire is F = I*L x B, where I is the current, L is the wire-length vector, and B is the magnetic field. AP Physics C E&M derives this from F = qv x B applied to a collection of moving charges in the wire. For a straight wire of length L in a uniform B: F = I*L*B*sin(theta). The 2018 FRQ tested this on a motor design - a current loop in B experiences forces on each side, producing torque tau = I*A x B. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct cross-product.

questions.prompt: Magnetic force does this on speed.
questions.explanation: Magnetic force does no work on a moving charge: F_magnetic = qv x B is always perpendicular to v (cross product property), so F . v = 0 and W = integral F . dv = 0. AP Physics C E&M tests this directly: the speed of a charged particle in a pure magnetic field is constant (KE unchanged); only the direction changes. The 2019 FRQ #2 on a cyclotron used this principle - the kinetic energy is gained from the alternating electric field between dees, not from the magnetic field which only redirects the particle. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining the no-work property.

questions.prompt: Trajectory in uniform B with v perpendicular.
questions.explanation: Trajectory of a charged particle in a uniform B with velocity v perpendicular to B is a circle, with radius determined by the balance between magnetic force qvB and centripetal force mv^2/r. AP Physics C E&M derives this: qvB = mv^2/r gives r = mv/(qB). The 2018 FRQ #2 tested this for a proton in B. The circle lies in the plane perpendicular to B, with axis along B. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining why pure magnetic field can only produce circular motion (no work, KE preserved, |v| constant).

Radius of circular motion in a magnetic field is r = m*v/(q*B), derived from qvB = mv^2/r (magnetic force = centripetal force). AP Physics C E&M uses this for cyclotron and mass-spectrometer analysis. Larger m or v gives larger r; larger q or B gives smaller r. The 2017 FRQ on a mass spectrometer tested this: different ions with the same kinetic energy curve to different radii based on m/q, separating isotopes. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct algebraic isolation of r.

Period of cyclotron motion is T = 2*pi*m/(q*B), the time for one full circular orbit in a uniform B. AP Physics C E&M derives this from T = 2*pi*r/v = 2*pi*(mv/qB)/v = 2*pi*m/(qB). Remarkably, T is independent of v - all particles of mass m and charge q orbit with the same period in a given B (the basis for the cyclotron design). The 2018 FRQ tested this for a particle accelerator. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining why this independence allows acceleration by an oscillating field at fixed frequency.

Trajectory with a parallel velocity component (v has both perpendicular and parallel-to-B components) is a helix, combining circular motion in the perpendicular plane with uniform linear motion along B. AP Physics C E&M analyzes this by decomposing v into v_perp (gives circle) and v_parallel (gives straight-line). The pitch (distance along B per revolution) is p = v_parallel * T = 2*pi*m*v_parallel/(qB). The 2019 FRQ tested this on a charged particle entering at an angle. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards velocity-decomposition reasoning.

questions.prompt: Selecting particle velocities with E and B.
choices: k q_1 q_2 over r squared
questions.explanation: A velocity selector uses crossed E and B fields to select particles of a specific velocity. AP Physics C E&M derives the selection condition: a particle moving through perpendicular E and B feels qE + qv x B, and only when qE = qvB (so v = E/B) is the net force zero, allowing it to pass through undeflected. Slower or faster particles deflect to one side or the other. The 2017 FRQ on a mass spectrometer's velocity selector tested this. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct force-balance setup; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining the selection mechanism.

questions.prompt: Law giving B field from current.
choices: Mu_0 I dl cross r-hat over 4 pi r squared
questions.explanation: The Biot-Savart law gives the magnetic field from a current element: dB = (mu_0/(4*pi))*(I*dl x r-hat)/r^2, where dl is the current-element vector and r-hat is the unit vector from dl to the field point. Discovered by Jean-Baptiste Biot and Felix Savart (1820). AP Physics C E&M uses this to compute B for current loops, solenoids, and arbitrary current distributions where Ampere's law symmetry fails. The 2018 FRQ #2 on a current loop tested integration of the Biot-Savart law. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct cross-product and integration.

choices: Four pi epsilon nought a b over (b minus a)
Mu_0 I dl cross r-hat over 4 pi r squared
questions.correctText: Mu_0 I dl cross r-hat over 4 pi r squared
questions.explanation: Biot-Savart formula in detail: dB = (mu_0/(4*pi))*(I*dl x r-hat)/r^2, where the cross product gives the direction (right-hand rule from current direction to field point). AP Physics C E&M integrates this over the current distribution to find total B. For a finite straight wire from x_1 to x_2: B = (mu_0*I/(4*pi*d))*(sin(theta_2) - sin(theta_1)), with d the perpendicular distance and theta_i the angles from the wire ends. The 2019 FRQ tested this. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct integration limits.

choices: Mu_0 I dl cross r-hat over 4 pi r squared
questions.explanation: Mu_0 (mu-naught) is the permeability of free space, with value 4*pi x 10^-7 T*m/A. AP Physics C E&M uses mu_0 in Biot-Savart, Ampere's law (closed integral B . dl = mu_0*I_enclosed), and Maxwell's equations. Together with epsilon_0 it determines the speed of light c = 1/sqrt(mu_0*epsilon_0) (Maxwell 1864). The 2018 FRQ tested mu_0 in the field-of-solenoid calculation. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) demands consistent unit handling; the value is exact by SI definition (until 2019 redefinition, when mu_0 became a measured quantity).

choices: Mu_0 I dl cross r-hat over 4 pi r squared
questions.explanation: Magnetic field at the center of a circular current loop of radius R is B = mu_0*I/(2*R). AP Physics C E&M derives this from the Biot-Savart law: each current element dl contributes dB = (mu_0/(4*pi))*(I*dl x r-hat)/r^2; by symmetry only the axial component survives integration; the loop circumference is 2*pi*R, and r = R for each element. Integration gives B = mu_0*I/(2*R) (axial direction). The 2018 FRQ #2 tested this. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards the symmetry-based integration.

questions.prompt: Line integral of B around loop.
choices: Closed integral B dl equals mu_0 I
questions.explanation: Ampere's law states that the line integral of B around a closed loop equals mu_0 times the enclosed current: closed integral B . dl = mu_0*I_enclosed. Discovered by Andre-Marie Ampere (1820s). AP Physics C E&M uses Ampere's law for highly symmetric problems where B can be pulled out of the integral: long straight wires, solenoids, toroids, cylindrical conductors. The 2018 FRQ #3 on a solenoid tested this. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards symmetry analysis before applying the law.

choices: Closed integral B dl equals mu_0 I
Mu_0 N I over 2 pi r
questions.correctText: Closed integral B dl equals mu_0 I
questions.explanation: Ampere's law equation: closed integral B . dl = mu_0*I_enclosed. AP Physics C E&M tests this on the 2017 FRQ #5: choose an Amperean loop that exploits symmetry (concentric with wire for cylindrical, rectangular for solenoid), compute the line integral (B*L for parallel B and dl), and equate to mu_0 times the enclosed current. The integral path is chosen to make B easy to compute. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct path choice; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards justifying when symmetry permits the simplification.

choices: Mu_0 N I over 2 pi r
Closed integral B dl equals mu_0 I
questions.explanation: Use of Ampere's law: solve for B from symmetry, when the geometry permits choosing an Amperean loop on which B is constant and parallel or perpendicular to dl everywhere. AP Physics C E&M uses Ampere's law for: long straight wires (concentric circle), infinite solenoid (rectangular loop), toroid (circle), infinite plane of current (rectangular loop), and infinite slab of current (rectangular loop). For non-symmetric problems Biot-Savart is required. The 2018 FRQ on a solenoid tested Ampere's law. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the symmetry argument.

Magnetic field inside an ideal long solenoid is B = mu_0*n*I, where n = N/L is the turns per unit length and I is the current. AP Physics C E&M derives this using Ampere's law with a rectangular Amperean loop spanning the solenoid wall: B*L = mu_0*(n*L)*I (left side: B inside times loop length; right side: enclosed current = (number of enclosed turns)*I = (n*L)*I). The 2018 FRQ #3 tested this. The field is uniform inside, nearly zero outside (idealized). Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards the loop choice.

choices: Mu_0 N I over 2 pi r
Closed integral B dl equals mu_0 I
questions.correctText: Mu_0 N I over 2 pi r
questions.explanation: Magnetic field inside a toroid (donut-shaped solenoid) is B = mu_0*N*I/(2*pi*r), where N is the total number of turns, I is the current, and r is the radial distance from the symmetry axis. AP Physics C E&M derives this using Ampere's law with a circular Amperean loop of radius r inside the toroid: B*(2*pi*r) = mu_0*N*I (the loop encloses all N turns), giving B = mu_0*N*I/(2*pi*r). The 1/r falloff inside the toroid reflects the curved geometry. The 2017 FRQ on a fusion-reactor design tested this. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct enclosure.

A moving charge creates a magnetic field. AP Physics C E&M treats this via the Biot-Savart law for current elements (collections of moving charges): dB = (mu_0/(4*pi))*(q*v x r-hat)/r^2 for a single moving point charge. This is the source side of electromagnetism, complementing the force side F = qv x B. The 2019 FRQ tested this concept: a stream of moving electrons creates B that can be detected by a nearby compass. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining how charges-at-rest produce only E, charges-in-motion produce both E and B.

Torque on a magnetic dipole in a uniform B field is tau = mu x B, with magnitude tau = mu*B*sin(theta) where theta is the angle between mu and B. The torque rotates the loop to align mu with B (minimum-PE orientation). AP Physics C E&M uses this for motor design: a loop carrying current I in field B experiences torque, accelerating its rotation. The 2017 FRQ on a motor tested this. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards cross-product; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the alignment trend (mu rotates toward B).

Permanent magnets owe their magnetism to aligned electron spins. AP Physics C E&M treats this conceptually: in ferromagnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt) the electron spins in adjacent atoms align spontaneously, creating domains of aligned magnetic moments. In an unmagnetized piece the domains are randomly oriented; in a magnetized piece they are largely aligned, producing a net macroscopic B. The 2019 FRQ tested the difference between paramagnetic (weak alignment) and ferromagnetic (strong, persistent alignment) materials. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the microscopic mechanism explanation.

Faraday's law states that the EMF induced in a circuit equals the negative time rate of change of magnetic flux through the circuit: EMF = -d(Phi_B)/dt. Discovered by Michael Faraday (1831) and independently by Joseph Henry (1832). AP Physics C E&M uses Faraday's law for generators, transformers, and induction problems. The 2018 FRQ on a loop in a changing B tested this. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct time-differentiation of flux; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the negative-sign (Lenz's law direction).

Faraday's law equation: EMF = -d(Phi_B)/dt, where Phi_B = integral B . dA is the magnetic flux through the surface bounded by the circuit. AP Physics C E&M tests this on the 2018 FRQ: a loop in a changing B field (or a loop being moved through B) has EMF = -d(Phi)/dt. The induced current I = EMF/R drives current that opposes the flux change (Lenz's law). Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct setup of Phi_B and time-derivative; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the sign analysis.

Magnetic flux through a surface is Phi_B = integral B . dA, where dA is the area-element vector perpendicular to the surface. For uniform B perpendicular to a flat surface of area A: Phi_B = B*A. AP Physics C E&M uses this in Faraday's law. The 2017 FRQ tested Phi_B for a tilted loop: Phi_B = B*A*cos(theta) with theta the angle between B and the loop normal. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards the dot-product computation; Practice 1 (representations) demands consistent orientation of the area vector.

The SI unit of magnetic flux is the weber (Wb), equal to 1 T*m^2 = 1 V*s. Named for Wilhelm Weber (1804-1891). AP Physics C E&M uses webers in Faraday's law: EMF = -d(Phi)/dt has units (Wb/s) = V. The dimensional consistency confirms the relationship. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) demands correct unit handling; Practice 4 (data analysis) rewards interpreting flux quantities in transformer and generator design.

Lenz's law states that the direction of induced current is such that its magnetic field opposes the change in flux that caused it. Heinrich Lenz (1834) generalized this from experimental observations. AP Physics C E&M uses Lenz's law to determine current direction without ambiguity. The 2019 FRQ on a magnet falling through a coil tested this: as the magnet enters, the induced current opposes the increasing flux, creating an upward force on the magnet (slowing its fall). Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining why Lenz's law is a consequence of energy conservation.

questions.prompt: EMF from rod moving in B.
questions.explanation: Motional EMF is the EMF induced in a conductor moving through a magnetic field. AP Physics C E&M derives it two ways: (1) from Faraday's law applied to a circuit with changing area, or (2) from the Lorentz force on charge carriers in the moving conductor (qv x B drives them along the wire). The 2018 FRQ tested this on a rod sliding on rails through B. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining the connection between Faraday's law (flux change) and Lorentz force (qv x B drives charges).

Motional EMF formula is EMF = B*L*v for a conductor of length L moving with velocity v perpendicular to B (with v also perpendicular to L). AP Physics C E&M derives this: force on each charge carrier is qvB, work per unit charge across length L is vBL, so EMF = BLv. The 2018 FRQ #3 on a rod on rails tested this: B = 0.5 T, L = 0.1 m, v = 2 m/s gives EMF = 0.1 V. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct geometry.

Current direction in a rod-rails circuit is determined by Lenz's law: the induced current must oppose the flux change. AP Physics C E&M tests this on the 2017 FRQ #4: a rod sliding right on rails through B (out of page) increases enclosed area and flux; the induced EMF drives current to create flux into the page (opposing increase), so current flows in the direction given by the right-hand rule (clockwise when viewed from outside). Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards Lenz's-law application.

Force on a moving conductor in a magnetic field, due to the induced current, opposes the conductor's motion. AP Physics C E&M derives this from F = I*L x B applied to the rod: the induced current I = BLv/R produces force F = BIL = B^2*L^2*v/R directed opposite to v. This is a manifestation of Lenz's law and energy conservation: work done against this force supplies the energy dissipated in the circuit's resistance. The 2018 FRQ #3 tested this. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the energy-conservation argument.

Power dissipated in a motional EMF circuit is P = I*EMF = (BLv)^2/R, equivalent to the mechanical power expended against the magnetic braking force. AP Physics C E&M tests this as an energy-conservation problem: P_input (mechanical, from whatever pulls the rod) = P_dissipated (electrical, in the resistor). The 2017 FRQ tested this for a sliding rod with measured terminal velocity. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the energy-balance interpretation.

choices: Mu_0 N squared A over L
questions.explanation: Self-inductance L is the property of a circuit (especially a coil) that opposes changes in current: EMF_induced = -L*(dI/dt). Joseph Henry discovered self-induction in 1832 (after whom the unit is named). AP Physics C E&M uses L for inductor analysis in RL and LC circuits. The 2018 FRQ #4 on an RL circuit tested EMF = -L*dI/dt. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct time-differentiation; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining the negative sign (Lenz's law - induced EMF opposes the current change).

choices: Mu_0 N squared A over L
questions.correctText: Mu_0 N squared A over L
questions.explanation: Self-inductance of a solenoid is L = mu_0*N^2*A/l, where N is the number of turns, A is the cross-sectional area, and l is the solenoid length. AP Physics C E&M derives this from L = N*Phi/I: B = mu_0*(N/l)*I (Ampere's law); Phi_per_turn = B*A = mu_0*(N/l)*I*A; total flux linkage = N*Phi = mu_0*N^2*A*I/l; so L = N*Phi/I = mu_0*N^2*A/l. The 2017 FRQ tested this. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct flux-linkage analysis.

LC oscillator angular frequency is omega = 1/sqrt(L*C). AP Physics C E&M derives this from the LC circuit ODE: L*d^2Q/dt^2 + Q/C = 0, with solutions Q(t) = Q_0*cos(omega*t + phi) and omega = 1/sqrt(LC). This is the electromagnetic analog of mass-spring SHM (omega = sqrt(k/m)). The 2018 FRQ on radio-frequency oscillator tested this. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards solving the ODE; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the analogy with mechanical SHM.

LC circuit oscillation: energy alternates between the inductor (KE-analog, (1/2)*L*I^2) and the capacitor (PE-analog, Q^2/(2C)) at angular frequency omega = 1/sqrt(LC). AP Physics C E&M treats this as the electromagnetic counterpart of mass-spring SHM. Total energy E = (1/2)*L*I^2 + Q^2/(2C) is conserved (ideal LC has no resistance). The 2019 FRQ tested energy exchange in an LC circuit. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards energy-exchange interpretation.

RL time constant is tau_RL = L/R, the characteristic time for current changes in an RL circuit. AP Physics C E&M derives this from the RL circuit ODE: L*dI/dt + I*R = EMF (charging) or L*dI/dt + I*R = 0 (decay), with solutions involving exp(-t*R/L) = exp(-t/tau). The 2018 FRQ #4 tested this with an inductor switched into a circuit. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards ODE solution; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the analogy with RC tau (R*C).

RL current after long time approaches I_final = EMF/R, the steady-state value where dI/dt = 0 (so V_L = 0 and the inductor acts like a wire). AP Physics C E&M derives this from the ODE L*dI/dt + IR = EMF: at steady state dI/dt = 0, so I = EMF/R. The 2017 FRQ tested this asymptotic value. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining why an inductor in DC steady state behaves like an ordinary wire (no induced EMF when current is constant).

questions.prompt: R-L current at t equals zero.
questions.explanation: RL current at t = 0 is zero, immediately after a switch closes. AP Physics C E&M derives this from the inductor's property: the induced EMF L*dI/dt opposes any abrupt current change. To go from zero to nonzero current instantly would require dI/dt = infinity, giving infinite back-EMF - impossible. Therefore I(0) = 0 and rises smoothly toward I_final = EMF/R. The 2018 FRQ #4 tested this initial condition. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the no-infinite-EMF argument.

Capacitive reactance is X_C = 1/(omega*C), the AC analog of resistance for a capacitor. AP Physics C E&M derives this from the relation V_C = Q/C and I = dQ/dt = omega*Q_max*cos(omega*t): the voltage across C lags I by 90 degrees, with amplitude V_C,max = (1/(omega*C))*I_max. The 2018 FRQ on an RC filter tested X_C. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct phasor analysis; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining why C blocks low frequencies (X_C -> infinity as omega -> 0).

Inductive reactance is X_L = omega*L, the AC analog of resistance for an inductor. AP Physics C E&M derives this from V_L = L*dI/dt: when I = I_max*sin(omega*t), V_L = omega*L*I_max*cos(omega*t), leading I by 90 degrees, amplitude V_L,max = omega*L*I_max. The 2017 FRQ on an inductive load tested X_L. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct phasor analysis; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining why L blocks high frequencies (X_L -> infinity as omega -> infinity).

choices: One over sqrt mu nought epsilon nought
questions.explanation: AC RMS value is I_rms = I_max/sqrt(2), the equivalent DC value that would dissipate the same average power. AP Physics C E&M derives I_rms from <I^2> = (1/T)*integral I^2 dt over one period: for sinusoidal I = I_max*sin(omega*t), <I^2> = I_max^2/2, so I_rms = sqrt(<I^2>) = I_max/sqrt(2). The 2018 FRQ tested this: 120 V_rms household AC has V_max = 170 V. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct time-averaging.

First Maxwell equation: Gauss's law for E in differential form: gradient . E = rho/epsilon_0. Equivalently in integral form: closed integral E . dA = Q_enclosed/epsilon_0. AP Physics C E&M treats this as the source equation for E - electric fields diverge from electric charges. Originally Gauss (1813), unified by Maxwell (1864). The 2018 FRQ on Maxwell's equations identified this as 'sources of E'. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining the physical meaning - electric charges are the sources and sinks of E.

Second Maxwell equation: Gauss's law for B: gradient . B = 0 (differential) or closed integral B . dA = 0 (integral). AP Physics C E&M treats this as the statement of no magnetic monopoles - magnetic field lines have no beginning or end, always forming closed loops. Any closed surface has zero net flux of B. The 2019 FRQ on Maxwell's equations tested this distinction from Gauss's law for E. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining the absence of magnetic monopoles - experimentally confirmed to extraordinary precision.

Third Maxwell equation: Faraday's law of induction: gradient x E = -d(B)/dt (differential) or closed integral E . dl = -d(Phi_B)/dt (integral). AP Physics C E&M treats this as the source equation linking changing B to E - a time-varying magnetic field induces an electric field. Originally Faraday (1831), unified by Maxwell (1864). The 2018 FRQ on Maxwell's equations identified this as 'changing B creates E'. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the physical meaning.

Fourth Maxwell equation: Ampere-Maxwell law: gradient x B = mu_0*J + mu_0*epsilon_0*(dE/dt) (differential), where J is current density and the new dE/dt term is the displacement current. Maxwell (1864) added the displacement-current term to Ampere's original law, fixing the inconsistency that arose in time-varying situations (charging capacitor). AP Physics C E&M treats this as the source equation linking changing E to B - completing the symmetry. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards Maxwell's correction.

questions.prompt: Scottish physicist unifying E and M.
choices: One over sqrt mu nought epsilon nought
questions.explanation: James Clerk Maxwell (1831-1879) was the Scottish physicist who unified the laws of electricity and magnetism into the four equations bearing his name (1864). His 'A Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism' (1873) predicted electromagnetic waves traveling at speed c = 1/sqrt(mu_0*epsilon_0), matching the measured speed of light - the first realization that light is an EM wave. AP Physics C E&M credits Maxwell as the founder of classical electrodynamics, comparable in stature to Newton in classical mechanics. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the unification narrative.

Displacement current is the term Maxwell added to Ampere's law in 1864: I_d = epsilon_0*d(Phi_E)/dt, accounting for changing electric flux as a source of magnetic field. AP Physics C E&M uses I_d to resolve the charging-capacitor paradox: between the plates of a charging capacitor there is no actual current (no charges flowing) but there is changing E, and Ampere's law with only the actual current I would give a contradiction. Maxwell's I_d term fixes this. The 2018 FRQ tested this. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards Maxwell's unification.

choices: Epsilon nought d Phi over dt
questions.correctText: Epsilon nought d Phi over dt
questions.explanation: Displacement current formula: I_d = epsilon_0*d(Phi_E)/dt, where Phi_E = integral E . dA is electric flux through a surface. AP Physics C E&M uses this in Ampere-Maxwell law: closed integral B . dl = mu_0*(I_conduction + I_d). The displacement current has the same effect on B as a real current - it's a source of magnetic field. The 2017 FRQ on a charging capacitor tested I_d between the plates. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct flux-derivative computation.

choices: Epsilon nought d Phi over dt
questions.explanation: Reason Maxwell added the displacement current: to maintain charge conservation (continuity equation) in the presence of time-varying currents. AP Physics C E&M derives this: without I_d, Ampere's law would be inconsistent for charging capacitors because the conduction current through a surface 'capping' the wire is non-zero while the current through a surface 'capping' between the plates is zero - violating conservation. With I_d the two cases agree. The 2018 FRQ tested this consistency argument. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards Maxwell's theoretical insight.

Modified Ampere law statement: closed integral B . dl = mu_0*(I_conduction + I_displacement) = mu_0*I_conduction + mu_0*epsilon_0*d(Phi_E)/dt. AP Physics C E&M treats this as the corrected fourth Maxwell equation, generalizing the original Ampere law for time-varying fields. The 2019 FRQ on EM waves tested this in the form gradient x B = mu_0*J + mu_0*epsilon_0*dE/dt for vacuum (J = 0) giving the wave equation. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct application.

Displacement current between capacitor plates equals the conduction current in the wires charging the capacitor. AP Physics C E&M derives this from charge conservation: the current I = dQ/dt that flows into one plate equals the rate of charge increase Q on that plate, which equals the rate of electric flux change Phi_E = Q/epsilon_0 (Gauss), so I = epsilon_0*d(Phi_E)/dt = I_d. The 2017 FRQ tested this equivalence. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the equivalence; Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards the derivation.

Electromagnetic waves are self-propagating disturbances of E and B fields, predicted by Maxwell in 1864 from his unified equations. AP Physics C E&M treats EM waves as transverse waves with E and B perpendicular to each other and to the propagation direction, traveling at speed c = 1/sqrt(mu_0*epsilon_0) ≈ 3 x 10^8 m/s in vacuum. Heinrich Hertz experimentally confirmed Maxwell's prediction (1887). The 2018 FRQ tested EM wave properties. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining the self-sustaining nature of EM waves.

Wavelength-frequency relation for EM waves is c = lambda*f, where lambda is wavelength, f is frequency, and c is the speed of light. AP Physics C E&M uses this throughout the EM spectrum: radio waves (lambda ~ 1 m, f ~ 300 MHz), microwaves (lambda ~ cm, f ~ GHz), visible (lambda ~ 500 nm, f ~ 6 x 10^14 Hz), gamma (lambda ~ 10^-12 m, f ~ 3 x 10^20 Hz). The 2018 FRQ tested this conversion. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct unit handling across wide ranges.

Visible light wavelengths span approximately 400 to 700 nanometers (violet to red), the narrow range of EM frequencies to which human eyes are sensitive. AP Physics C E&M treats this as a small slice of the broader EM spectrum: ultraviolet just below 400 nm, infrared just above 700 nm. The 2019 FRQ tested this distinction. Different colors correspond to different wavelengths: red ~ 700 nm, orange ~ 620, yellow ~ 580, green ~ 530, blue ~ 470, violet ~ 420. Science Practice 4 (data analysis) rewards reading the EM spectrum diagram.

Radio waves have the lowest frequencies (and longest wavelengths) of the EM spectrum, ranging from kHz to GHz. AP Physics C E&M categorizes: extremely low frequency (ELF, 3-30 Hz), audio (30 Hz - 30 kHz), AM radio (530 kHz - 1.7 MHz), FM radio (88 - 108 MHz), TV (54 - 700 MHz), microwave (3 - 300 GHz). The 2018 FRQ tested AM/FM bands. Science Practice 4 (data analysis) rewards range identification; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining why radio waves require large antennas (lambda comparable to antenna size).

Gamma rays have the highest frequencies (and shortest wavelengths) of the EM spectrum, with f > 10^19 Hz and lambda < 10^-11 m. AP Physics C E&M treats gamma rays as emerging from nuclear processes (radioactive decay, nuclear reactions) and cosmic events (supernovae, AGN). They carry energies hf > 100 keV, sufficient to ionize tissue and damage DNA. The 2017 FRQ tested gamma vs X-ray distinction. Science Practice 4 (data analysis) rewards numerical range.

Infrared (IR) radiation is the EM band carrying heat, with wavelengths from 700 nm to 1 mm and frequencies from THz to PHz. AP Physics C E&M treats IR as emitted thermally by all objects above absolute zero (blackbody radiation, Stefan-Boltzmann 1879-1884). Astronomy uses IR to see through dust; medicine uses IR thermography to detect inflammation; military uses IR for night-vision. The 2018 FRQ tested IR characterization. Science Practice 4 (data analysis) rewards range identification.

X-rays are used for medical and security imaging: penetrating biological soft tissue while being absorbed by dense materials (bones, metals). Wilhelm Roentgen discovered X-rays in 1895 (1901 Nobel Prize). AP Physics C E&M categorizes X-rays as 10^16 - 10^19 Hz, lambda 10^-11 - 10^-8 m. Medical X-rays at ~30-150 keV; airport security at lower energies. The 2019 FRQ tested X-ray imaging principles. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining penetration based on photon energy and material absorption.

choices: Half epsilon nought E squared plus half B squared over mu nought
questions.explanation: Poynting vector S = (1/mu_0)*E x B points in the direction of EM wave propagation, perpendicular to both E and B. AP Physics C E&M uses S to track energy flow: |S| has units W/m^2 = power per area = intensity. The direction is determined by the right-hand rule: point fingers from E to B, thumb gives propagation direction. The 2019 FRQ tested S geometry. Science Practice 1 (representations) demands consistent right-hand-rule application.

choices: E cross B over mu nought
questions.correctText: E cross B over mu nought
questions.explanation: Poynting vector formula is S = (1/mu_0)*E x B (in SI units; some texts include 4*pi factor in Gaussian units). AP Physics C E&M uses this to compute energy flow in EM waves. For a plane wave traveling in the x-direction with E_y and B_z (E perpendicular to B, both perpendicular to propagation): S = E*B/mu_0 in the x-direction. Time-averaged intensity I = <S> = E_max^2/(2*mu_0*c). The 2018 FRQ tested this. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct cross product.

choices: E cross B over mu nought
questions.explanation: Intensity of an EM wave is the time-averaged magnitude of the Poynting vector: I = <|S|> = (1/2)*epsilon_0*c*E_max^2 = E_max^2/(2*mu_0*c). AP Physics C E&M uses this for sunlight intensity (~1370 W/m^2 at Earth's orbit), laser-beam intensities, and broadcast-power calculations. The 2019 FRQ tested this. Science Practice 4 (data analysis) rewards interpreting intensity values; Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct time-averaging.

Radiation pressure on a perfectly absorbing surface is P_rad = I/c, where I is the EM wave intensity. AP Physics C E&M derives this from momentum carried by EM radiation: dp/dt = (energy/c) per unit time, so pressure = momentum flux density = I/c. For a perfectly reflecting surface the pressure doubles to 2*I/c. The 2017 FRQ tested this on solar-sail propulsion. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the radiation-pressure mechanism.

Coulomb's law F = k*q_1*q_2/r^2 is the foundational force law for electrostatics, with k = 8.99 x 10^9 N*m^2/C^2 = 1/(4*pi*epsilon_0). Charles-Augustin de Coulomb (1785) discovered the inverse-square law using a torsion balance. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses this for point-charge interactions, paralleling Newton's gravitational law but ~10^36 times stronger between same-mass particles. The 2018 FRQ #1 on a system of three point charges tested vector superposition: F_total on each charge is the vector sum of pairwise Coulomb forces. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct vector decomposition; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards sign analysis (like repels, unlike attracts).

Electric field E = F/q is the force per unit charge at a point - a vector field independent of any test charge placed in it. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review tests this as the fundamental field quantity from which V, U, and force on test charges all derive: F = qE, U = qV, E = -gradient of V. For a point charge Q: E = k*Q/r^2 radially. The 2017 FRQ tested superposition of E from multiple sources (vector sum at the field point). Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards distinguishing E (field property, source-defined) from F (force on a test charge, F = qE); Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct vector summation.

Gauss's law relates electric flux through a closed surface to the enclosed charge: closed integral E . dA = Q_enclosed/epsilon_0. Gauss (1813) formulated this as a powerful equivalent to Coulomb's law for symmetric geometries. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses this for symmetric problems (spherical, cylindrical, planar) where E can be pulled out of the integral. The 2018 FRQ #1 on a uniformly charged sphere tested both interior (E ~ r, depends on enclosed charge inside) and exterior (E ~ 1/r^2, behaves as point charge) regions. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards symmetry analysis before applying; Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct Gaussian-surface choice.

Electric potential V = U/q is the potential energy per unit charge, a scalar field with SI units volts (V = J/C). AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses V as a scalar field that simplifies energy calculations: U = qV, W_ext = q*delta V, E = -gradient of V. For a point charge: V = k*Q/r (with V = 0 at infinity). The 2019 FRQ tested both V from a charge distribution (V = sum k*q_i/r_i) and the relationship E = -gradient of V to recover E from V. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the scalar advantage over vector E - scalars add without direction; Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct line-integral computation.

Electric field is the negative gradient of potential: E = -gradient of V (3D vector form, or E_x = -dV/dx in 1D). AP Physics C E&M cumulative review tests both directions: V to E (differentiate to find slope of potential surface) and E to V (integrate field along path). The 2018 FRQ tested both: from V(x) students computed E_x = -dV/dx, then the force on a test charge q. The negative sign means E points from high V toward low V (positive charges accelerate down potential hills). Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct partial differentiation; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the physical interpretation - E is the gradient of the potential surface.

Ohm's law V = I*R for ohmic conductors, with V the potential difference, I the current, and R the resistance. Georg Ohm (1827) discovered this linear relation in his German monograph. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses Ohm's law throughout DC circuit analysis. The 2018 FRQ on a multi-resistor network combined Ohm's law with Kirchhoff's rules for full analysis. Power dissipated is P = V*I = I^2*R = V^2/R - three equivalent forms. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct application; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards distinguishing ohmic (V-I linear, R constant) from non-ohmic conductors (diodes, where V-I is exponential).

choices: One over sqrt mu nought epsilon nought
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL, junction rule): sum of currents entering a node equals sum leaving, i.e., Sigma I = 0 with signs. Gustav Kirchhoff (1845) derived this from charge conservation - charge cannot accumulate at a junction. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses KCL at every circuit node. The 2019 FRQ tested KCL on a multi-loop circuit: at each junction, write Sigma I_in = Sigma I_out. Combined with KVL (loop rule from energy conservation) and Ohm's law, KCL completes the toolkit for any DC circuit. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards connecting KCL to charge conservation; Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards solving simultaneous equations.

Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL, loop rule): sum of voltage drops around a closed loop equals zero, i.e., Sigma delta V = 0. Kirchhoff (1845) derived this from energy conservation - going around a loop must give zero net potential difference. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses KVL on each loop in multi-loop circuits, accounting for sign: EMF positive when traversed from - to +, IR drops negative when traversed in current direction. The 2018 FRQ tested KVL combined with KCL for complete analysis. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct sign tracking; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the energy-conservation interpretation.

RC time constant is tau = R*C, with SI units seconds (Omega*F = (V/A)*(C/V) = s). AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses tau to characterize charging/discharging exponentials: V(t) = V_0*exp(-t/RC) (discharge) or V(t) = V_0*(1 - exp(-t/RC)) (charge). After tau the capacitor is at 63.2% of final state; after 5*tau, 99.3%. The 2018 FRQ #3 tested this on an RC circuit. Derived from the first-order ODE R*dQ/dt + Q/C = EMF, the exponential time-dependence is characteristic. Science Practice 3 (graphical analysis) rewards reading tau from V-t plots; Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct ODE solution.

Capacitance is C = Q/V, the ratio of stored charge to applied voltage, with SI unit farad (F = C/V). Named for Michael Faraday. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review tests C for various geometries: parallel plates C = epsilon_0*A/d, spherical C = 4*pi*epsilon_0*(a*b)/(b-a), cylindrical C = 2*pi*epsilon_0*L/ln(b/a). The 2017 FRQ tested parallel-plate capacitance with given A, d. Energy stored is U = (1/2)*C*V^2 = Q^2/(2C). Combination rules: parallel C_total = C_1 + C_2 (add directly); series 1/C_total = 1/C_1 + 1/C_2 (reciprocal sum). Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct geometric scaling; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the field-storage interpretation.

Lorentz force F = q*v x B on a moving charge in a magnetic field, named for Hendrik Lorentz (1853-1928). AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses this for cyclotron motion (circular paths with r = mv/(qB)), mass spectrometers (separation by m/q), velocity selectors (E and B crossed). The magnetic force is perpendicular to v, so it changes direction but not speed - no work is done on the charge. The 2018 FRQ tested this on a particle entering a uniform B. Science Practice 1 (representations) demands right-hand-rule conventions; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards explaining the no-work property - the speed of a charged particle in a pure magnetic field is constant.

Biot-Savart law gives B from a current element: dB = (mu_0/(4*pi))*(I*dl x r-hat)/r^2. Discovered by Jean-Baptiste Biot and Felix Savart (1820) immediately after Orsted's discovery (1819) that currents produce magnetic fields. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses this for current loops (B_center = mu_0*I/(2*R)), finite-length wires, and arbitrary current distributions where Ampere's law symmetry fails. The 2017 FRQ tested integration over a circular loop - by symmetry only the axial component survives. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct setup and integration; Practice 1 (representations) demands right-hand-rule for dB direction.

questions.prompt: Line integral B equals mu nought I.
questions.explanation: Ampere's law: closed integral B . dl = mu_0*I_enclosed, discovered by Andre-Marie Ampere (1820s). AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses this for symmetric problems (long straight wires, solenoids, toroids, infinite cylindrical conductors) where B can be pulled out of the integral. The 2018 FRQ #3 on a solenoid tested this: rectangular Amperean loop spanning the wall gives B*L = mu_0*n*L*I, so B = mu_0*n*I inside. For long straight wires: B*(2*pi*r) = mu_0*I gives B = mu_0*I/(2*pi*r). Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards symmetry analysis; Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards Amperean-loop choice.

Magnetic field inside a long solenoid is B = mu_0*n*I, where n = N/L is the turns per unit length. Derived from Ampere's law with a rectangular Amperean loop spanning the solenoid wall: B*L = mu_0*(n*L)*I, where (n*L)*I = number of enclosed turns times current. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review tests this on the 2018 FRQ #3 with numerical values. The field is uniform inside and nearly zero outside (idealized infinite solenoid). The 2017 FRQ extended this to toroids: B = mu_0*N*I/(2*pi*r) (1/r falloff inside the donut shape). Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct enclosed-current calculation; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the symmetry assumption.

Radius of circular motion for a charged particle in a magnetic field is r = m*v/(q*B), derived from balancing magnetic force qvB with centripetal force mv^2/r. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses this for cyclotron and mass-spectrometer analysis: different ions with the same KE follow different r based on m/q (the mass-to-charge ratio), enabling isotope separation. The 2019 FRQ tested this with numerical values: a proton in B = 1 T at v = 10^6 m/s gives r = m*v/(qB) ≈ 1 cm. Period T = 2*pi*m/(qB) is independent of v - all particles of same m, q have same orbital period (basis of the cyclotron). Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct algebraic isolation.

Faraday's law: EMF = -d(Phi_B)/dt, induced EMF equals the negative time rate of change of magnetic flux. Michael Faraday (1831) discovered this experimentally; Joseph Henry (1832) independently. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses this for generators (rotating loops in B), transformers (changing primary current induces secondary EMF), induction motors, wireless charging. The 2018 FRQ on a loop in a changing B field tested d(Phi_B)/dt with both B(t) and area A potentially time-varying. The negative sign (Lenz's law) gives the direction of induced current. Science Practice 2 (mathematical routines) rewards correct time-differentiation; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards Lenz's-law direction analysis.

Lenz's law: the direction of induced current is such that its magnetic field opposes the flux change that caused it. Heinrich Lenz (1834) formulated this. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses Lenz's law (encoded in the negative sign of Faraday's law) to determine current direction unambiguously. The 2019 FRQ on a magnet falling through a coil tested this: entering the coil, induced current opposes the increasing flux, creating an upward force that slows the magnet. Energy conservation underlies Lenz's law - the mechanical work done against this force is exactly the electrical energy dissipated in the coil. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the energy-conservation interpretation.

questions.prompt: Scottish physicist unifying E and M.
questions.explanation: James Clerk Maxwell (1831-1879), Scottish physicist, unified electricity and magnetism with his four equations (1864), predicting EM waves at speed c = 1/sqrt(mu_0*epsilon_0) - the speed of light. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review honors Maxwell as the founder of classical electrodynamics, comparable in stature to Newton in classical mechanics. His Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism (1873) established the field-theory framework, demonstrating that light is an EM wave - a prediction confirmed by Hertz in 1887. The 2018 FRQ tested student recognition of Maxwell's contribution. Science Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the unification narrative.

choices: Mu_0 N I over 2 pi r
questions.explanation: Poynting vector S = (1/mu_0)*E x B points in the direction of EM wave propagation, perpendicular to both E and B. AP Physics C E&M cumulative review uses S to track energy flow in EM waves: |S| has units W/m^2 (intensity). The direction is determined by the right-hand rule: point fingers from E to B, thumb gives propagation direction. For a plane wave |S_avg| = E_max^2/(2*mu_0*c) = (1/2)*epsilon_0*c*E_max^2 is the time-averaged intensity. The 2018 FRQ tested this geometry on sunlight (~1370 W/m^2 at Earth's orbit). Science Practice 1 (representations) demands right-hand-rule conventions; Practice 6 (argumentation) rewards the energy-flow interpretation.
